---
title: |
  Mathematical Foundations of AI & ML<br>Unit 12: Neural Networks from First Principles
bibliography: ref.bib
author:
  - name: Prof. Dr. Philipp Pelz
    affiliation:
      - FAU Erlangen-Nürnberg
execute:
  eval: false
format:
  revealjs:
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    template-partials:
      - title-slide.html
    css: custom.css
    theme: custom.scss
    slide-number: c/t
    logo: "eclipse_logo_small.png"
    footer: "© Philipp Pelz - Mathematical Foundations of AI & ML"
---

## 01. Title: Neural Networks from First Principles
- Unit scope and role.

## 02. Learning outcomes
- Derive and interpret basic neural network training.

## 03. Recap from Unit 11
- Probabilistic modeling and loss link.

## 04. Why neural networks
- Representation learning motivation.

## 05. Single neuron model
- Affine map + nonlinearity.

## 06. Activation functions
- ReLU, sigmoid, tanh roles.

## 07. Multi-layer perceptron structure
- Depth and width intuition.

## 08. Function composition view
- Hierarchical feature building.

## 09. Forward pass equations
- Layer-by-layer computation.

## 10. Loss function setup
- Regression vs classification objectives.

## 11. Computational graph idea
- Dependencies for differentiation.

## 12. Gradient-based training objective
- Parameter update framing.

## 13. Chain rule refresher
- Core backprop mechanism.

## 14. Backpropagation intuition
- Error signal propagation.

## 15. Backprop for output layer
- Gradient structure.

## 16. Backprop for hidden layers
- Recursive gradient flow.

## 17. Matrix-form implementation
- Efficient vectorized training.

## 18. SGD and mini-batch updates
- Practical optimization loop.

## 19. Learning rate effects
- Convergence and instability.

## 20. Initialization strategies
- Symmetry breaking and scale.

## 21. Vanishing/exploding gradients
- Depth-related failure modes.

## 22. Normalization techniques
- Training stabilization.

## 23. Regularization in NNs
- Weight decay, dropout, early stopping.

## 24. Generalization in deep models
- Capacity vs data regime.

## 25. Universal approximation intuition
- Expressivity perspective.

## 26. Bias-variance in NNs
- Practical interpretation.

## 27. Optimization landscape (light)
- Non-convex but trainable.

## 28. Batch size tradeoffs
- Noise and hardware efficiency.

## 29. Loss surface diagnostics
- Monitoring training behavior.

## 30. Overfitting diagnostics
- Train/val gap signals.

## 31. Calibration of neural nets
- Confidence reliability issue.

## 32. Interpreting learned features
- Hidden representations.

## 33. MLP vs CNN vs Transformer preview
- Architectural landscape.

## 34. Hardware considerations
- Compute and memory scaling.

## 35. Reproducibility checklist
- Seeds, splits, configs.

## 36. Materials example: tabular property prediction
- MLP baseline design.

## 37. Materials example: image descriptor learning
- Feature learning context.

## 38. Materials example: spectral regression
- 1D network framing.

## 39. Common failure mode #1
- Poor normalization.

## 40. Common failure mode #2
- Data leakage in pipeline.

## 41. Common failure mode #3
- Overconfident outputs.

## 42. Mitigation checklist
- Robust NN workflow.

## 43. Exercise scaffold: implement 2-layer MLP
- Forward + backward pass.

## 44. Exercise scaffold: train with SGD
- Learning-rate sweep.

## 45. Exercise scaffold: regularization comparison
- L2/dropout/early stop.

## 46. Exercise scaffold: calibration check
- Reliability analysis.

## 47. Exam-oriented key statements
- High-yield concepts.

## 48. Summary table
- Building blocks and pitfalls.

## 49. Unit summary
- Core takeaways.

## 50. References + transition
- Unit 13 explainability/robustness bridge.

::: {#refs}
:::
